title: Vietnam War
description: Test your skills on the causes and the effects of the Vietnam War!

assessment_items.question_content:
> “It is important to United States security interests that all practicable measures be taken to prevent further communist expansion in Southeast Asia. Indo-China is a key area of Southeast Asia and is under immediate threat. The neighboring countries of Thailand and Burma could be expected to fall under Communist domination if a Communist-dominated government controlled Indo-China. The balance of Southeast Asia would then be in grave hazard.” 

>-Source: Draft Report by the National Security Council, 1950

**The author’s views expressed in the excerpt best reflect a foreign policy built around:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: containing the influence of the Soviet Union.
expanding peacekeeping efforts in Asia.
facilitating decolonization in Southeast Asia.
reducing nuclear arsenals around the world.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “It is important to United States security interests that all practicable measures be taken to prevent further communist expansion in Southeast Asia. Indo-China is a key area of Southeast Asia and is under immediate threat. The neighboring countries of Thailand and Burma could be expected to fall under Communist domination if a Communist-dominated government controlled Indo-China. The balance of Southeast Asia would then be in grave hazard.” 

>-Source: Draft Report by the National Security Council, 1950

**The ideas expressed in the passage most strongly influenced which United States foreign policy decision in the 1960s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: sending troops to Vietnam to overthrow the rising Communist government
joining the North Atlantic Treaty Organization to ensure collective security
placing sanctions on and ending trade with Southeast Asia and the Soviet Union
withholding military resources from Thailand and Burma until the defeat of Communist-led governments

assessment_items.question_content:
> “It is important to United States security interests that all practicable measures be taken to prevent further communist expansion in Southeast Asia. Indo-China is a key area of Southeast Asia and is under immediate threat. The neighboring countries of Thailand and Burma could be expected to fall under Communist domination if a Communist-dominated government controlled Indo-China. The balance of Southeast Asia would then be in grave hazard.” 

>-Source: Draft Report by the National Security Council, 1950

**Which of the following developments in the 1960s best represented the continuation of the ideas expressed in the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: supporting non-Communist regimes in Latin America
sending military resources to South Korea in the Korean War
creating free trade agreements with China and the Soviet Union
setting up arms limitation treaties with the Soviet Union

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The integrity of the U.S. commitment is the principal pillar of peace throughout the world. If that commitment becomes unreliable, the communist world would draw conclusions that would lead to our ruin and almost certainly to a catastrophic war. So long as the South Vietnamese are prepared to fight for themselves, we cannot abandon them without disaster to peace and to our interests throughout the world.” 

>-Source: Secretary of State Dean Rusk, 1965

**The excerpt most directly reflects the United States efforts to:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: limit the spread of communism.
support decolonization.
negotiate an armistice.
return to isolationism.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The integrity of the U.S. commitment is the principal pillar of peace throughout the world. If that commitment becomes unreliable, the communist world would draw conclusions that would lead to our ruin and almost certainly to a catastrophic war. So long as the South Vietnamese are prepared to fight for themselves, we cannot abandon them without disaster to peace and to our interests throughout the world.” 

>-Source: Secretary of State Dean Rusk, 1965

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of a Communist leader in Northern Vietnam
the Soviet military invasion of Southern Vietnam
the decision by the United Nations to sanction the Soviet Union
the start of communist protests in the Vietnamese capital

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The integrity of the U.S. commitment is the principal pillar of peace throughout the world. If that commitment becomes unreliable, the communist world would draw conclusions that would lead to our ruin and almost certainly to a catastrophic war. So long as the South Vietnamese are prepared to fight for themselves, we cannot abandon them without disaster to peace and to our interests throughout the world.” 

>-Source: Secretary of State Dean Rusk, 1965

**Which of the following United States actions most directly resulted from the goals expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: sending troops and military resources to South Vietnam
training Vietnamese troops in the United States
donating funds to the Southern Vietnamese army
negotiating peace talks between the warring countries

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a13bd2177dccdd75ea5b278618889dff.jpg)

>-Source: Joaquin De Alba Carmona, “What’s the price of the apple?” [Wikimedia Commons,](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ho_Chi_Mihn_What%27s_the_price_of_the_apple.jpg) 1965

**Which of the following most directly led to the circumstances illustrated by the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of independence movements in Asia against colonialism
the attempts by the French to reclaim colonies in Indo-China
the physical devastation after the United States launched atomic bombs
the United States’ entry into the civil war in Korea

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a13bd2177dccdd75ea5b278618889dff.jpg)

>-Source: Joaquin De Alba Carmona, “What’s the price of the apple?” [Wikimedia Commons,](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ho_Chi_Mihn_What%27s_the_price_of_the_apple.jpg) 1965

**Which of the following developments is the most direct effect of the situation portrayed in the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States supported South Vietnam.
American forces withdrew from Vietnam.
Vietnam developed a nuclear arsenal.
The Soviet Union acquired Vietnam as a territory.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a13bd2177dccdd75ea5b278618889dff.jpg)

>-Source: Joaquin De Alba Carmona, “What’s the price of the apple?” [Wikimedia Commons,](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ho_Chi_Mihn_What%27s_the_price_of_the_apple.jpg) 1965

**The developments referenced by the image most directly contributed to which of the following United States actions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: sending troops to Vietnam to support the Southern Vietnamese forces
offering North Vietnam financial assistance and arms to resist Soviet aggression
petitioning the United Nations to punish the Soviet Union’s actions
declaring war on Vietnam and the Soviet Union to contain communism

assessment_items.question_content:
> “When the Constitutional Convention was debating allocation of the war power, George Mason of Virginia said that he was against giving the power of war to the Executive, because not safely to be trusted with it; or to the Senate, because not so constructed as to be entitled to it. He was for clogging rather than facilitating war; but ‘for facilitating peace.’ . . . 
> “Not only in the policies we have permitted at least two successive Presidents to pursue, but in institutional arrangements that Congress and the people have acquiesced in for some decades now, we have managed to reverse the proper order of things. We have managed to clog peace and facilitate war. I think it is time we got back on course.” 

>-Source: Alexander Bickel, Yale Law Professor, Testimony before Congress, [Congressional Record,](https://books.google.com/books?id=Ng-bHTct9GsC&pg=PA27654&lpg=PA27654&dq#v=onepage&q&f=false)1971

**The ideas expressed in the excerpt emerged most directly from a larger intellectual debate over the:**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: expansion of presidential powers.
lack of diverse representation in Congress.
reduction in the minimum age to vote.
military intervention in underdeveloped countries.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “When the Constitutional Convention was debating allocation of the war power, George Mason of Virginia said that he was against giving the power of war to the Executive, because not safely to be trusted with it; or to the Senate, because not so constructed as to be entitled to it. He was for clogging rather than facilitating war; but ‘for facilitating peace.’ . . . 
> “Not only in the policies we have permitted at least two successive Presidents to pursue, but in institutional arrangements that Congress and the people have acquiesced in for some decades now, we have managed to reverse the proper order of things. We have managed to clog peace and facilitate war. I think it is time we got back on course.” 

>-Source: Alexander Bickel, Yale Law Professor, Testimony before Congress, [Congressional Record,](https://books.google.com/books?id=Ng-bHTct9GsC&pg=PA27654&lpg=PA27654&dq#v=onepage&q&f=false)1971

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the United States’ entry into the Vietnam War
the disarmament negotiations with the Soviet Union
the Supreme Court’s ruling *United States v. Nixon*
the institution of the draft to send troops to Vietnam

assessment_items.question_content:
> “When the Constitutional Convention was debating allocation of the war power, George Mason of Virginia said that he was against giving the power of war to the Executive, because not safely to be trusted with it; or to the Senate, because not so constructed as to be entitled to it. He was for clogging rather than facilitating war; but ‘for facilitating peace.’ . . . 
> “Not only in the policies we have permitted at least two successive Presidents to pursue, but in institutional arrangements that Congress and the people have acquiesced in for some decades now, we have managed to reverse the proper order of things. We have managed to clog peace and facilitate war. I think it is time we got back on course.” 

>-Source: Alexander Bickel, Yale Law Professor, Testimony before Congress, [Congressional Record,](https://books.google.com/books?id=Ng-bHTct9GsC&pg=PA27654&lpg=PA27654&dq#v=onepage&q&f=false)1971

**Which of the following federal actions most directly supported the ideas expressed in the excerpt? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: passage of the War Powers Act
ratification of the Twenty-sixth Amendment
elimination of executive orders
impeachment of Richard Nixon
